justification: All pass criteria met: MD5 identified as cryptographically broken with specific impact (forgeable tokens, NIST deprecation); HMAC-SHA256 explicitly recommended as replacement; timing attack via === clearly identified with exploitation method; crypto.timingSafeEqual recommended with proper Buffer usage; timestamp design flaw identified as a critical functional break (token unvalidatable); safe_to_deploy false. Additionally caught bonus issues: length-extension vulnerability from concatenation pattern, missing expiry enforcement, and partnerId delimiter injection.

observations.text: MD5 flagged as Critical with concrete attack scenario (brute-force timestamp within 30-day window to recover secret)
Correctly distinguishes hash-then-concatenate vs proper HMAC keying as a separate important issue (length-extension attack)
Timing attack clearly explained with iterative character-guessing reproduction steps
crypto.timingSafeEqual recommended with the practical note about length-check guard
Timestamp problem identified not just as a design flaw but as a complete functional break — tokens are unvalidatable
Actionable fix suggestions including structured token format and JWT alternative
Caught the partnerId delimiter injection issue — a subtle but real concern
Production readiness section correctly identifies this as fundamentally broken with appropriate migration considerations
